title: MUS 347 Spring Break Quiz
instructions: Answer all questions. Closed notes. For audio questions, listen to the example and identify what you hear.

questions.prompt: Listen to Audio Example 1. Which vowel frequency is being boosted?

media.title: Audio Example 1
media.caption: Listen carefully, then select the vowel frequency being boosted.

media.title: Audio Example 2
media.caption: Listen carefully, then select the vowel frequency being boosted.

media.title: Audio Example 3
media.caption: Listen to the processing being applied.

media.caption: Listen to the processing being applied.

media.title: Audio Example 5

questions.prompt: EQ is used primarily to adjust:
questions.explanation: EQ (equalization) shapes the frequency content of a signal, which we perceive as changes in tone color or timbre.

questions.prompt: Another name for a "low cut" filter is:
questions.explanation: A low cut removes low frequencies, meaning it lets high frequencies pass through — hence "high-pass filter."

questions.prompt: A pass filter's slope (e.g., 12 dB/octave vs. 24 dB/octave) describes:

choices.label: How steeply frequencies are attenuated past the cutoff

questions.explanation: Slope describes how aggressively frequencies are reduced beyond the cutoff point. A steeper slope (e.g., 24 dB/oct) removes frequencies more sharply than a gentle one (e.g., 6 dB/oct).

questions.prompt: A shelf filter's curve "goes up/down then ____":
questions.explanation: A shelf filter boosts or cuts all frequencies above (or below) a set point and then stays at that level — like a shelf on a bookcase.

questions.prompt: Which filter type allows only a "window" of middle frequencies to pass through?
questions.explanation: A bandpass filter combines a high-pass and a low-pass, letting only a middle band of frequencies through while attenuating both highs and lows.

questions.prompt: Why do engineers apply a low cut (high-pass) to most tracks?

choices.label: To remove rumble/bleed and clear low-frequency space

questions.explanation: Most tracks contain inaudible low-end rumble, mic handling noise, or bleed that eats up headroom. A high-pass filter cleans this up without affecting the useful content of the signal.

questions.prompt: The "Q" parameter on a parametric EQ controls:

choices.label: The slope of a pass filter

questions.explanation: Q (quality factor) determines how wide or narrow the affected frequency band is. A high Q targets a very narrow range; a low Q affects a broad range.

questions.prompt: Match the frequency to its vowel: 2,000 Hz =
questions.explanation: The standard vowel-frequency associations are: 250 Hz = oo, 500 Hz = o, 1k Hz = ah, 2k Hz = a, 4k Hz = ee.

questions.prompt: A boost that makes vocals sound "honky" or "nasal" is likely around:
questions.explanation: The 800 Hz–1.2 kHz range is often associated with a "honky" or nasal quality. Boosting here can make vocals sound boxy or telephone-like.

questions.prompt: A boost that adds "chestiness" or "mud/thickness" is likely around:
questions.explanation: The 200–300 Hz range adds body and chest resonance. Too much boost here creates a muddy or boomy quality.

questions.prompt: Unlike EQ, dynamics processors respond to:
questions.explanation: EQ works on frequency content, while dynamics processors (compressors, gates, limiters) react to how loud the signal is — its amplitude.

questions.prompt: Compression typically results in:
questions.explanation: A compressor reduces the gap between the loudest and softest parts of a signal, resulting in a smaller (reduced) dynamic range.

questions.prompt: Which processor would you use to reduce background noise between vocal phrases?
questions.explanation: A gate silences (or reduces) signal that falls below its threshold, making it ideal for cutting noise in the gaps between vocal phrases.

questions.prompt: If a compressor's GR meter shows 0 dB, what does that indicate?

choices.label: The threshold is too high to measure

questions.explanation: GR stands for Gain Reduction. When it reads 0 dB, no gain is being reduced — the signal is below the threshold and the compressor is not acting.

questions.prompt: A limiter prevents the signal from going:

choices.label: Above a set level (acts as a ceiling)

questions.explanation: A limiter is essentially a compressor with an extreme ratio. It sets a hard ceiling that the signal cannot exceed.

questions.prompt: Why should you disable the master limiter while making mixing decisions?

choices.label: It can mask problems and affect your perception of dynamics

questions.explanation: A master limiter makes everything sound louder and more even, which can hide mix problems. Mixing without it gives you an honest picture of your dynamics and balance.

questions.prompt: In Gibson's 3D mix model, left/right position is controlled by:
questions.explanation: Gibson's model describes three dimensions of a mix: left/right (panning), top/bottom (frequency/EQ), and front/back (volume and reverb). Panning controls the left-right placement.

questions.prompt: When two sounds "mask" each other, it means:

choices.label: One sound hides or obscures the other due to frequency/spatial overlap

questions.explanation: Masking occurs when two sounds occupy similar frequency ranges and stereo positions, causing one to hide or obscure the other.

questions.prompt: A stereo file contains:
questions.explanation: Stereo means two channels — Left and Right. This is how most music is delivered to listeners.

questions.prompt: A potential problem with the Haas effect is:

choices.label: It can cause comb filtering/phase issues when summed to mono

questions.explanation: The Haas effect uses a very short delay between left and right channels. When these are summed to mono, the delay causes phase cancellation (comb filtering), which can hollow out the sound.

questions.prompt: In a dynamics processor, what does "threshold" mean?

choices.label: The maximum output level a speaker can handle
The amplitude level at which the processor begins to affect the signal
The frequency where highs begin to roll off
The time it takes for the processor to react

questions.explanation: The threshold is the level at which a dynamics processor "turns on." Signal below threshold passes unaffected; signal that crosses it is processed.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly compares threshold in a compressor versus a gate?

choices.label: A compressor reduces signal below threshold, while a gate reduces signal above threshold
A compressor and gate both reduce signal only above threshold in exactly the same way
A compressor reduces signal above threshold, while a gate reduces signal below threshold
A compressor ignores threshold, but a gate uses it

questions.explanation: A compressor acts on signal that goes ABOVE the threshold (turning loud parts down). A gate acts on signal that falls BELOW the threshold (silencing quiet parts).

questions.prompt: What is the difference between pan and balance?

choices.label: Pan adjusts a mono source within a stereo field, while balance adjusts the relative level of left and right in a stereo signal
Pan and balance mean exactly the same thing
Pan is for volume, while balance is for EQ
Pan sends right to left and left to right, while balance only works on mono tracks

questions.explanation: Pan places a mono signal at a position in the stereo field. Balance adjusts the relative loudness of the left and right channels in an already-stereo signal.

questions.prompt: In mixing, what does masking mean?

choices.label: A track has too much stereo width
A sound is hidden or covered up by another sound
A signal is clipped by a limiter
A channel is muted by a gate

questions.explanation: Masking happens when one sound obscures another because they overlap in frequency, timing, or stereo position. Fixing masking is a core part of mixing.

questions.prompt: Why can the Haas effect cause problems with mono compatibility?

choices.label: It makes the signal too quiet in stereo
It adds reverb that disappears in mono
It uses a very short delay between channels, which can create phase cancellation or comb filtering when summed to mono
It always pans both channels to the center

questions.explanation: The Haas effect relies on a short delay between L and R. When collapsed to mono, the delayed copy partially cancels the original, creating comb filtering that thins out the sound.
